body: “You ran from us once,” he murmured. “You won’t do it again.” Four princes want me. Two of them touched me in the dark and never forgot 🔥. One charms me like sunlight. One calms me like a lullaby. And the last two look at me like prey they already claimed 😰. Every dance, every hunt, every stolen moment tightens the noose around my neck. Because if they discover the truth growing inside me… the game won’t end in courtship. It will end in blood 🩸❤️‍🔥. Chapter 1 POV Eva The SUV cuts through the city at dusk, and I slump against the leather seats like something boneless, still flushed and sweaty from dance class.  My leotard clings uncomfortably beneath my jacket, a second skin I can't wait to shed. But that discomfort is nothing compared to the weight pressing down on my chest—the echo of my father's voice still ringing in my ears. Legacy. Bloodline.  Heirs responsibility of the werewolf dynasty, which they teach before you can walk. I've heard the sermon a thousand times. The same words, the same disappointment barely hidden beneath his measured tone. Twelve generations of Aldridge power, and it all rests on my shoulders.  Shoulders that apparently aren't strong enough to carry it. "He acts like I chose this," I mutter, catching my bodyguard Dominic's eyes in the rearview mirror. "Like I woke up one morning and decided. You know what? I don't want a wolf. Being broken sounds like fun." Nic's knuckles tighten on the steering wheel, but he doesn't respond.  He never does when I vent about my father.  His wolf paces beneath his silence—I can see it in the slight tension of his jaw, the way his shoulders hold themselves rigid. He's alert to my frustration even when he can't fix it. I'm twenty-two years old now. Twenty-two and wolfless. The Aldridge pack heir whose beast never woke, whose eyes have never glowed gold, who has never run beneath the moon or joined a pack hunt.  Every other wolf my age shifted years ago. They remember their first transformation like teenagers remember their first kiss—a milestone, a becoming.  I remember only doctors' offices and council's prayers and the hollow space inside my chest where something wild should live. My entire existence has been one long performance of compensation. Perfect grades. Perfect posture. Perfect Alpha princess, making up for the imperfection I can't hide.  And I'm exhausted. "Do you know I've never been to a real party?"  The words tumble out before I can stop them.  "Not once. Not ever. I've attended galas and balls and formal dinners where everyone watches me like I'm a specimen under glass, but I've never just... gone out. Never danced somewhere my father's eyes couldn't follow." Dominic's gaze flicks to the mirror again.  "Clubs are overrated." His voice is carefully neutral, the tone he uses when he's trying to protect me from myself. "Just sweaty people making bad decisions." "Maybe I want to make a bad decision." I lean forward between the seats, close enough that he can probably scent my skin—warm and human and achingly familiar. Close enough to see the muscle jump in his cheek.  "Just five minutes, Nic. That's all I'm asking. I just want to know what it feels like to be nobody. To walk into a room where no one bows or stares or whispers about the broken princess." "Eva." My name sounds like a warning on his lips. "Please." I let the word hang there, fragile and honest. "I need this. Just once, I need to know what it's like to be invisible." He says no.  His jaw tightens, and he says no again, eyes fixed on the road ahead.  I watch the battle play out across his features—duty warring with something softer, something he never lets himself name. My hand brushes his shoulder, barely a touch, and I feel the moment his resolve fractures. He changes lanes without another word. The underground district rises around us like a different world. Graffiti blooms across warehouse walls. Neon flickers in windows that shouldn't have lights.  Dominic navigates the streets with a familiarity that surprises me—how many times has he been here without me knowing? I wonder until he pulls into an unmarked alley beside a building that pulses with muffled bass. The club is hidden beneath a warehouse, the entrance nothing more than a steel door and a bored bouncer. Werewolves and humans mingle in the line outside, anonymous in the darkness.  No one glances twice at the SUV.  No one knows a princess sits inside, heart hammering with anticipation. Dominic turns to face me, and his expression is torn in ways I've never seen before. Duty and softness war in his dark eyes, and for a moment, I think he might change his mind.  Drive me home. Lock me in my gilded cage where I belong. "Five minutes,” he says instead. “You stay where I can see you. Then we leave." I agree too quickly, already reaching for the door handle. The night air hits my face like a revelation—cool and sharp, carrying the scent of exhaust and rain and freedom. Inside, the club swallows me whole. Bass pounds through my chest like a second heartbeat, so loud I can feel it in my teeth. Bodies move in the strobing darkness, a sea of limbs and heat and want.  Wolves with barely glowing eyes grind against humans who don't know the danger pressed against them. The air is thick with sweat and alcohol and something wild.  Something primal that calls to the hollow space inside me. No one bows. No one stares. No one whispers behind their hands about the wolfless princess who should have been more. For the first time in my life, I am invisible.  I am nobody. I am free. The realization crashes over me like a wave, and I want to laugh, want to cry, want to spin in circles until the room blurs into streaks of color.  I've spent twenty-two years being watched, being judged, being found wanting. And here, in this sweaty underground world, I'm just another body in the crowd. Just another girl chasing sensation. I turn to share the moment with Dominic, to grab his arm and point at the beautiful chaos surrounding us, but he's gone. My heart stutters. I scan the crowd, searching for his broad shoulders, his familiar silhouette, but the sea of bodies has swallowed him completely. Panic flickers before understanding dawns. He's searching for me the only way he knows how. By scent. A scent I don't have. Without a wolf, I'm untraceable. My body doesn't broadcast itself the way other wolves' do.  I move through the world scentless, invisible, a ghost even to those with supernatural senses. It's always been my greatest shame—evidence of my brokenness, my incompleteness. But tonight, it's my freedom. Dominic can't find me. Not here, not in this chaos of competing scents and pounding music. For the first time in my life, I have slipped my leash entirely. I turn to face the bar, my heart pounding with something that feels dangerously like excitement.  The lights catch in crystal glasses, in the glitter on strangers' skin, in the gold-glowing eyes of wolves who look right through me. Tonight, I'm not a princess. Tonight, I'm just a girl in a club full of strangers. And I'm not ready to leave.  Chapter 2 The bar glows like a beacon through the chaos, and I push toward it with newfound determination.  Bodies part around me without acknowledgment, just another anonymous girl in the crowd, and I drink in anonymity like oxygen. A woman nearby holds something pink and luminescent, the glass catching the strobing lights like liquid magic.  I don't know what it is and I don't really care.  I catch the bartender's eye and point at it, my voice swallowed by the bass before it leaves my throat. He nods, understanding, and slides an identical glass across the scarred wood without asking for ID or questions. The first sip is sweet and sharp, burning a pleasant path down my throat. I've had champagne at galas, wine at formal dinners, always under my father's watchful eye, always measured and appropriate.  This is different. This tastes like rebellion. A stranger beside me signals for another round, and when the bartender delivers, the man pushes a second glass toward me with a lazy smile.  He says something I can't hear over the music, but his meaning is clear enough.  A gift. No strings. Just the easy generosity of someone who doesn't know my name or my bloodline or my shame. I drink both glasses because I've never built a tolerance. Because my father would be horrified. Because tonight I don't care about the consequences, and maybe I never will again.  The alcohol hits fast and hard, flooding my empty stomach, rushing to my head like the tide coming in.  The club's harsh lights blur into something soft and dreamy, edges smoothing until the whole world feels wrapped in gauze. I lean toward the bartender, emboldened by the warmth spreading through my chest. He's older, world-weary, the kind of man who's heard every confession the darkness has to offer. "This might be my only night of freedom," I tell him, and my words come out slightly slurred, loose in ways they've never been. "I need to make it unforgettable. Something I'll remember forever." He studies me for a long moment. His eyes are human, no wolf lurking behind them, but they're sharp with the kind of knowing that comes from watching people at their most vulnerable.  Then he slides another drink across the counter—something darker this time, amber liquid that glows like trapped fire. "On the house," he says. "For courage." I drink it without hesitation, letting the burn chase away the last of my inhibitions. The alcohol hits harder now, three drinks deep with nothing in my stomach. My head swims pleasantly, the club's harsh lights blurring into something soft and dreamy.  My body moves in ways dance class never taught me—fluid and instinctive, as if the rhythm has crawled inside my bones. I'm spinning through the crowd like I finally belong. The hollow space inside my chest doesn't feel empty anymore.  It feels full of music and color and the press of strangers who see nothing when they look at me except a girl worth touching. Two men find me in the chaos. I sense them before I see them—a shift in the air, a weight in my chest that has nothing to do with the alcohol.  They're tall, both of them. One broader with dark features, a shadow given form, all sharp edges and dangerous beauty. The other leaner, pale angles cutting through the darkness like frost on glass.  In the strobing lights, I can barely make out their faces. Just impressions. Sharp jaws and hungry eyes and mouths that look like they know secrets I want to learn. But I feel their presence like a physical force. The power radiating off them is unmistakable, pressing against my skin even from a distance. Alphas. The broader one reaches me first, sliding into my space like he belongs there. His hand finds my hip, steadying me when I sway. "Careful, little one." His voice is silk and sin, some Mediterranean accent curling around the words. "You'll fall." "Maybe I want to fall," I hear myself say, bold in ways I've never been. His laugh is low and dark. "Is that so?" The leaner one appears at my other side, his presence cold where the other is heat. "She's drunk." His voice is clipped, Nordic. Disapproving. "I'm free," I correct him, tilting my head back to meet his pale gaze. "There's a difference." Something flickers in those frost-colored eyes. Interest, maybe. Or hunger. "Is there?" "I've never been drunk before." I'm babbling now, the alcohol loosening my tongue. "Never been to a club. Never danced with strangers. Never done anything that wasn't planned and approved and…"  I gesture vaguely, nearly losing my balance. "Controlled." The broader one's grip tightens on my hip. "A virgin to freedom, then." "To everything that matters." The words hang between us, heavier than I intended. The two men exchange a look over my head—a silent conversation I can't decipher. "And what do you want?" the leaner one asks, his cold voice dropping to something rougher. "Tonight. Right now. What do you want?" I should lie. Should deflect. Should remember that I'm a princess and these are strange Alphas and nothing good can come from this. Instead, I tell the truth. "I want to feel something real. I want one night where I'm not empty." The broader one leans closer, his breath warm against my ear. "We can help with that." Hands find my hips. My waist. I'm sandwiched between them before I register the movement, their bodies pressed against mine from front and back as we move to the bass.  The broader one's chest is a solid heat against my spine. The leaner one's fingers grip my hip bones like he's already claiming them.  I'm drowning in sensation, in the cedar and smoke of one scent mingling with the frost and pine of another, in the knowledge that two of the most powerful creatures in this room want me. The moment shatters when I spot Dominic. He's pushing through the crowd maybe twenty feet away, his face tight with panic. Even from here, I can see his eyes flashing amber as his wolf surges toward the surface.  He's spotted me. He's coming. My freedom is about to end. The thought cuts through the haze like ice water, and I make a decision without thinking. My hands find both men's wrists, my grip desperate despite the drugs softening my edges. "Help me." The words come out breathless, pleading. "Get me out of here. Please." They don't ask questions. They don't hesitate. The broader one wraps an arm around my waist, pulling me against him. The leaner one turns and cuts a path through the crowd, his presence alone making bodies part like water around a stone.  Within seconds we're through a back exit I never noticed, tumbling into the cool night air. The temperature shocks my heated skin, but there's no time to adjust. A taxi idles at the curb like fate placed it there. The three of us pile inside, and I'm sandwiched between two wolves whose names I never asked. Whose faces I still can't quite focus on. Whose scents are wrapping around me like promises of everything I've been denied. The taxi pulls away from the curb just as the back door bursts open behind us. Dominic stands in the alley, chest heaving, eyes blazing amber—but he's too late. I'm already gone.  One moment of freedom starts a chain reaction that can’t be stopped ⛓️Read Princes’ Lover now and discover how one night of freedom changes everything 🔥

body:
« Tu as fui loin de nous une fois, » murmura-t-il. « Tu ne recommenceras pas. »

Quatre princes me veulent. Deux d’entre eux m’ont effleurée dans l’obscurité et n’ont jamais oublié 🔥. L’un me charme comme un rayon de soleil. L’autre m’apaise comme une berceuse. Et les deux derniers me regardent comme une proie qu’ils ont déjà revendiquée 😰.

Chaque danse, chaque chasse, chaque instant volé resserre le nœud coulant autour de mon cou. Car s’ils découvrent la vérité qui grandit en moi… le jeu ne se terminera pas par une cour. Il se terminera dans le sang 🩸❤️‍🔥.

Chapitre 1
POV Eva

Le SUV fend la ville au crépuscule, et je m’effondre contre les sièges en cuir comme une chose sans os, encore rougie et moite après le cours de danse. Mon justaucorps colle inconfortablement sous ma veste, une seconde peau que je meurs d’envie d’ôter. Mais ce malaise n’est rien comparé au poids qui écrase ma poitrine—l’écho de la voix de mon père résonne encore dans mes oreilles.

Héritage. Lignée. Héritiers, responsabilité de la dynastie des loups-garous, qu’ils t’enseignent avant même que tu ne saches marcher. J’ai entendu le sermon mille fois. Les mêmes mots, la même déception à peine dissimulée sous sa voix mesurée. Douze générations de puissance Aldridge, et tout repose sur mes épaules.

Des épaules qui, apparemment, ne sont pas assez fortes pour porter tout cela.

« Il agit comme si j’avais choisi tout ça, » je marmonne, croisant le regard de mon garde du corps Dominic dans le rétroviseur. « Comme si je m’étais réveillée un matin et décidé. Tu sais quoi ? Je ne veux pas de loup. Être brisée, ça a l’air amusant. »

Les jointures de Nic blanchissent sur le volant, mais il ne répond pas. Il ne répond jamais quand je me plains de mon père.

Son loup rôde sous son silence—je le vois dans la tension subtile de sa mâchoire, la façon dont ses épaules restent raides. Il perçoit ma frustration même s’il ne peut rien y changer.

J’ai vingt-deux ans maintenant. Vingt-deux ans et toujours sans loup.

L'héritière de la meute Aldridge dont la bête ne s'est jamais réveillée, dont les yeux n'ont jamais brillé d'or, qui n'a jamais couru sous la lune ni rejoint une chasse de meute. Tous les autres loups de mon âge se sont transformés il y a des années. Ils se souviennent de leur première transformation comme les adolescents se souviennent de leur premier baiser—un moment marquant, une révélation. Moi, je ne me souviens que des cabinets médicaux, des prières du conseil et de ce vide dans ma poitrine où quelque chose de sauvage devrait vivre. Toute mon existence a été une longue performance pour compenser. Notes parfaites. Maintien parfait. Parfaite princesse Alpha, rattrapant l’imperfection que je ne peux cacher.

Et je suis épuisée.

« Sais-tu que je ne suis jamais allée à une vraie fête ? »

Les mots m’échappent avant que je puisse les retenir.

« Pas une seule fois. Jamais. J’ai assisté à des galas et des bals et des dîners formels où tout le monde me regarde comme un spécimen sous cloche, mais je ne suis jamais juste... sortie. Jamais dansé quelque part où les yeux de mon père ne pouvaient pas me suivre. »

Le regard de Dominic se pose à nouveau sur le miroir.

« Les boîtes de nuit sont surfaites. » Sa voix est soigneusement neutre, ce ton qu’il prend quand il essaie de me protéger de moi-même. « Juste des gens en sueur qui prennent de mauvaises décisions. »

« Peut-être que j’ai envie de prendre une mauvaise décision. » Je me penche entre les sièges, assez près pour qu’il puisse sans doute sentir mon odeur—chaleureuse, humaine, terriblement familière. Assez près pour voir le muscle tressauter dans sa joue.

« Juste cinq minutes, Nic. C’est tout ce que je demande. Je veux juste savoir ce que ça fait d’être personne. D’entrer dans une pièce où personne ne s’incline, ne me fixe, ou ne murmure à propos de la princesse brisée. »

« Eva. » Mon nom sonne comme un avertissement sur ses lèvres.

« S’il te plaît. » Je laisse le mot flotter, fragile et honnête. « J’en ai besoin. Juste une fois, j’ai besoin de savoir ce que ça fait d’être invisible. »

Il dit non.

Sa mâchoire se serre et il répète non, les yeux fixés sur la route devant lui.

J'observe la bataille se dérouler sur ses traits — le devoir livrant combat à quelque chose de plus doux, quelque chose qu'il ne s'autorise jamais à nommer. Ma main effleure son épaule, à peine un contact, et je sens le moment où sa résolution se fissure. Il change de voie sans un mot de plus. Le quartier souterrain s'élève autour de nous comme un autre monde. Des graffitis fleurissent sur les murs des entrepôts. Le néon vacille aux fenêtres qui ne devraient pas être éclairées.

Dominic navigue dans les rues avec une familiarité qui me surprend — combien de fois est-il venu ici sans que je le sache ? Je me pose la question jusqu'à ce qu'il s'engage dans une ruelle sans nom, à côté d'un bâtiment vibrant d'une basse étouffée. Le club est caché sous un entrepôt, l'entrée n'est rien de plus qu'une porte d'acier et un videur blasé. Loups-garous et humains se mêlent dans la file à l'extérieur, anonymes dans l'obscurité.

Personne ne jette un second regard au SUV. Personne ne sait qu'une princesse est assise à l'intérieur, le cœur battant d'anticipation. Dominic se tourne vers moi, et son expression est déchirée comme jamais auparavant. Devoir et tendresse s'affrontent dans ses yeux sombres, et pendant un instant, je crois qu'il pourrait changer d'avis. Ramène-moi à la maison. Enferme-moi dans ma cage dorée, là où je devrais être.

"Cinq minutes," dit-il finalement. "Tu restes là où je peux te voir. Ensuite, on part." J'acquiesce trop vite, déjà la main sur la poignée de la porte. L'air nocturne me frappe au visage comme une révélation — frais et incisif, portant le parfum des gaz d'échappement, de la pluie et de la liberté. À l'intérieur, le club m'engloutit tout entière. La basse martèle ma poitrine comme un second battement de cœur, si fort que je la ressens jusque dans mes dents. Les corps se meuvent dans l'obscurité stroboscopique, une mer de membres, de chaleur et de désir.

Des loups aux yeux à peine luisants se frottent aux humains qui ne connaissent pas le danger pressé contre eux. L'air est épais de sueur, d'alcool et de quelque chose de sauvage.

Quelque chose de primal résonne dans le vide en moi. Personne ne s'incline. Personne ne fixe du regard. Personne ne murmure derrière sa main à propos de la princesse sans loup qui aurait dû être plus. Pour la première fois de ma vie, je suis invisible. Je ne suis personne. Je suis libre. La réalisation me submerge comme une vague, et j'ai envie de rire, de pleurer, de tourner sur moi-même jusqu'à ce que la pièce se dissolve en traînées de couleurs.

J'ai passé vingt-deux ans à être observée, jugée, trouvée insuffisante. Et ici, dans ce monde souterrain et moite, je ne suis qu'un corps de plus dans la foule. Juste une autre fille en quête de sensations. Je me tourne pour partager cet instant avec Dominic, pour saisir son bras et lui montrer le chaos magnifique qui nous entoure, mais il a disparu. Mon cœur rate un battement. Je scrute la foule, cherchant ses larges épaules, sa silhouette familière, mais la mer de corps l'a entièrement englouti. La panique vacille avant que la compréhension ne s'impose. Il me cherche de la seule manière qu'il connaisse. Par l'odeur. Une odeur que je ne possède pas. Sans loup, je suis intraçable. Mon corps ne se signale pas comme celui des autres loups.

Je traverse le monde sans odeur, invisible, un fantôme même pour ceux qui possèdent des sens surnaturels. Cela a toujours été ma plus grande honte—la preuve de ma brisure, de mon incomplétude. Mais ce soir, c'est ma liberté. Dominic ne peut pas me retrouver. Pas ici, pas dans ce chaos d'odeurs qui se mêlent et de musique assourdissante. Pour la première fois de ma vie, j'ai entièrement glissé hors de ma laisse. Je me tourne vers le bar, le cœur battant d'une émotion qui ressemble dangereusement à de l'excitation.

Les lumières s'accrochent dans les verres de cristal, dans les paillettes sur la peau des inconnus, dans les yeux dorés et luisants des loups qui me traversent du regard. Ce soir, je ne suis pas une princesse. Ce soir, je suis juste une fille dans un club plein d'étrangers. Et je ne suis pas prête à partir.

Chapitre 2

Le bar luit comme un phare à travers le chaos, et je m’y dirige avec une détermination nouvelle. Les corps s’écartent autour de moi sans un mot, je ne suis qu’une fille anonyme dans la foule, et je respire l’anonymat comme de l’oxygène. Près de moi, une femme tient quelque chose de rose et lumineux, le verre captant les lumières stroboscopiques comme une magie liquide. Je ne sais pas ce que c’est, et je m’en fiche. J’attrape le regard du barman et je pointe le doigt vers le verre, ma voix engloutie par la basse avant même de s’élever. Il hoche la tête, comprend sans mot dire, et fait glisser un verre identique sur le bois marqué sans demander de pièce d’identité, sans poser de questions. La première gorgée est douce et piquante, traçant une brûlure agréable dans ma gorge. J’ai déjà bu du champagne lors de galas, du vin à des dîners officiels, toujours sous le regard vigilant de mon père, toujours de façon mesurée et convenable. Ceci est différent. Cela a le goût de la rébellion.

Un inconnu à côté de moi commande une autre tournée, et quand le barman sert, l’homme pousse un second verre vers moi avec un sourire paresseux. Il dit quelque chose que je n’entends pas à cause de la musique, mais son intention est limpide. Un cadeau. Sans condition. Juste la générosité simple de quelqu’un qui ne connaît ni mon nom, ni mon sang, ni ma honte.

Je bois les deux verres parce que je n’ai jamais bâti de tolérance. Parce que mon père serait horrifié. Parce que ce soir, je me moque des conséquences, et peut-être que plus jamais je ne m’en soucierai. L’alcool frappe vite et fort, inonde mon estomac vide, me monte à la tête comme la marée montante. Les lumières crues du club se brouillent, deviennent douces, oniriques, les contours s’estompent jusqu’à ce que le monde entier semble enveloppé de gaze.

Je me penche vers le barman, enhardie par la chaleur qui se répand dans ma poitrine. Il est plus âgé, marqué par la vie, du genre à avoir entendu toutes les confessions que la nuit peut offrir.

« C’est peut-être ma seule nuit de liberté », je lui dis, et mes mots sortent légèrement brouillés, relâchés d’une façon qu’ils n’ont jamais eue. « Je dois la rendre inoubliable. Quelque chose dont je me souviendrai toujours. » Il m’observe longuement. Ses yeux sont humains, aucun loup n’y sommeille, mais ils sont aiguisés de cette lucidité née d’avoir vu les gens à leur plus grande vulnérabilité.

Puis il fait glisser un autre verre sur le comptoir — quelque chose de plus sombre cette fois, un liquide ambré qui luit comme du feu prisonnier. « C’est la maison qui offre », dit-il. « Pour le courage. » Je le bois sans hésiter, laissant la brûlure dissiper ce qui me restait d’inhibitions. L’alcool me frappe plus fort maintenant, déjà trois verres sans rien dans l’estomac. Ma tête tourne agréablement, les lumières crues du club se fondent en quelque chose de doux et vaporeux.

Mon corps bouge d’une façon que les cours de danse ne m’ont jamais enseignée — fluide et instinctive, comme si le rythme avait rampé jusque dans mes os. Je virevolte à travers la foule, comme si j’y avais enfin ma place. Le vide creusé dans ma poitrine ne paraît plus aussi creux.

Il est rempli de musique, de couleurs, de la pression des inconnus qui, en me regardant, ne voient rien d’autre qu’une fille qui mérite d’être touchée. Deux hommes me trouvent dans le chaos. Je les sens avant de les voir — un changement dans l’air, un poids dans la poitrine, sans rapport avec l’alcool.

Ils sont grands, tous les deux. L’un, plus massif, aux traits sombres, une ombre incarnée, tout en angles tranchants et beauté dangereuse. L’autre, plus élancé, des lignes pâles qui découpent l’obscurité comme le givre sur une vitre.

Dans les éclats de lumière stroboscopique, je distingue à peine leurs visages. Juste des impressions. Mâchoires acérées, yeux affamés, bouches qui semblent détenir des secrets que je veux apprendre. Mais je ressens leur présence comme une force physique.

La puissance qui émane d’eux est incontestable, pressant contre ma peau même à distance. Des Alphas. Le plus large m’atteint en premier, s’insinuant dans mon espace comme s’il y avait toujours appartenu. Sa main trouve ma hanche, me stabilisant quand je vacille. « Doucement, petite. » Sa voix est de la soie et du péché, un accent méditerranéen enroulé autour des mots. « Tu vas tomber. »

« Peut-être que j’en ai envie, » je m’entends répondre, audacieuse comme jamais auparavant. Son rire est grave, obscur. « Vraiment ? »

Le plus mince apparaît de mon autre côté, sa présence froide là où l’autre est chaleur. « Elle est ivre. » Sa voix est brève, nordique. Désapprobatrice.

« Je suis libre, » je le corrige, penchant la tête en arrière pour croiser son regard pâle. « Il y a une différence. »

Quelque chose vacille dans ces yeux couleur de givre. De l’intérêt, peut-être. Ou de la faim. « Vraiment ? »

« Je n’ai jamais été ivre avant. » Je bafouille maintenant, l’alcool déliant ma langue. « Jamais mis les pieds dans un club. Jamais dansé avec des inconnus. Jamais rien fait qui n’ait été planifié, approuvé et… »

Je fais un geste vague, manquant presque de perdre l’équilibre. « Contrôlé. »

La poigne du plus large se resserre sur ma hanche. « Vierge de la liberté, alors. »

« De tout ce qui compte. » Les mots flottent entre nous, plus lourds que je ne l’aurais voulu.

Les deux hommes échangent un regard au-dessus de ma tête — une conversation silencieuse que je ne peux déchiffrer.

« Et que veux-tu ? » demande le plus mince, sa voix froide se faisant plus rocailleuse. « Ce soir. Maintenant. Que veux-tu ? »

Je devrais mentir. Détourner la question. Me rappeler que je suis une princesse et qu’ils sont des Alphas étrangers, que rien de bon ne peut en sortir. Au lieu de ça, je dis la vérité.

« Je veux ressentir quelque chose de vrai. Je veux une nuit où je ne suis pas vide. »

Le plus large se penche plus près, son souffle chaud contre mon oreille. « On peut t’aider pour ça. »

Des mains trouvent mes hanches. Ma taille.

Je suis prise en sandwich entre eux avant même de percevoir le mouvement, leurs corps pressés contre le mien, devant et derrière, tandis que nous nous laissons porter par la basse. Le torse du plus large est une chaleur solide contre ma colonne vertébrale. Les doigts du plus mince agrippent mes os des hanches comme s'il me revendiquait déjà. Je me noie dans les sensations, dans le cèdre et la fumée de l’un de leurs parfums mêlés au givre et au pin de l’autre, dans la conscience que deux des créatures les plus puissantes de cette pièce me désirent. L’instant se brise quand j’aperçois Dominic. Il fend la foule à peut-être six mètres, le visage crispé par la panique. Même d’ici, je vois ses yeux briller d’ambre alors que son loup surgit à la surface. Il m’a repérée. Il arrive. Ma liberté est sur le point de prendre fin. La pensée transperce la brume comme une eau glacée, et je prends une décision sans réfléchir. Mes mains attrapent les poignets des deux hommes, ma prise désespérée malgré la drogue qui émousse mes sens. « Aidez-moi. » Les mots sortent dans un souffle, suppliants. « Sortez-moi d’ici. S’il vous plaît. » Ils ne posent pas de questions. Ils n’hésitent pas. Le plus large passe un bras autour de ma taille, me tirant contre lui. Le plus mince se retourne et trace un chemin à travers la foule, sa seule présence faisant s’ouvrir les corps comme l’eau autour d’une pierre.

En quelques secondes, nous franchissons une sortie arrière que je n’avais jamais remarquée, débouchant dans la fraîcheur nocturne. La température choque ma peau brûlante, mais je n’ai pas le temps de m’y habituer. Un taxi attend au bord du trottoir comme si le destin l’y avait placé. Nous nous entassons tous les trois à l’intérieur, et je suis à nouveau prise en sandwich entre deux loups dont je n’ai jamais demandé les noms. Dont je n’arrive toujours pas à fixer les visages. Dont les parfums m’enveloppent comme des promesses de tout ce qui m’a été refusé. Le taxi s’éloigne du trottoir au moment même où la porte arrière claque derrière nous. Dominic se tient dans la ruelle, la poitrine haletante, les yeux flamboyant d’ambre—mais il est trop tard. Je suis déjà partie.

body:
“She’s just the first wife,” his sister said. “The new one will outshine her.”
 They thought I’d crumble when he announced his second marriage. Instead, I rose 🌹. For years, I swallowed their insults, their cruelty, the loneliness of a palace that never felt like home 💔. But I’m not the powerless girl they married off. I’m the daughter of King Dorian. The true heir to Verdana 🔥. And now that my crown is no longer hidden, I’m not here to beg for love—I’m here to take back everything they tried to steal 🔥👑.
 
 CHAPTER 1: 
 ELARA’S POV 
 The crystal champagne flute trembled in my hand as I watched my husband of three years move through the crowd like a predator among prey. Polished and pristine in his midnight blue formal attire, Prince Caden Verloren was the picture of royal perfection—a masterpiece I once believed I had some part in creating. "You've barely touched your drink, Your Highness," Lady Mirabelle whispered, her voice dripping with false concern. "Is something troubling you this evening?" I forced my lips into the practiced smile that had become as much a part of my royal wardrobe as the heavy crown that often adorned my head. "Not at all," I lied. "Just savoring the moment." What a bitter thing to savor. The Grand Hall of Verloren Palace glittered with more jewels and secrets than stars in the night sky. I'd grown accustomed to both during my years as Caden's wife, though neither brought me comfort anymore. "He looks especially handsome tonight," another courtier remarked, her eyes following my husband's movements with poorly disguised hunger. "The future king in his element." "Indeed," I replied, the single word nearly choking me. I remembered a time when Caden's smile had been reserved for me alone. When his hand would find mine beneath the table during tedious state dinners. When his eyes would seek mine across crowded rooms just like this one, a silent promise that we were in this together. Those days had vanished like morning mist before the summer sun. "Elara." My mother-in-law's voice cut through my thoughts. Queen Arielle approached with the silent grace of a viper. "Do fix your posture, dear. A queen never slumps." "I'm not a queen, Your Majesty," I reminded her, though I straightened my spine nonetheless. "Merely a princess." Her lips thinned to a bloodless line. "For now. But appearances matter at all times. Especially tonight." The cryptic addition sent a chill down my perfectly straight spine. "What's special about tonight?" I asked, trying to keep my voice light. The Queen's smile didn't reach her eyes. "You'll see soon enough." Before I could press further, she glided away, leaving me with a growing knot of dread in my stomach. I set down my untouched champagne and made my way toward Caden, determined to extract some explanation. I nearly reached him when the King's steward struck his staff against the marble floor three times. The resounding crack silenced the room immediately. "His Royal Majesty, King Darius Verloren the Third!" The crowd parted as my father-in-law entered, Caden immediately moving to stand beside him. My husband's eyes swept the room but passed over me as though I were merely another courtier. I felt my cheeks burn. "Valued guests and noble friends," the King began, his booming voice filling every corner of the vast hall. "We gather tonight not only to celebrate the prosperity of our kingdom but to share joyous news." My breath caught in my throat. Could it be the northern treaty? Caden had been working for months to secure peace with our traditional enemies. If successful, it would mean an end to centuries of bloodshed. The King's hand came to rest on Caden's shoulder with unusual affection. "Prince Caden has secured an alliance that will strengthen our realm for generations to come." Pride swelled in my chest momentarily. So he had done it. Despite the personal distance between us, I couldn't help but admire his diplomatic skill. "Through the ancient and honorable practice of royal marriage," the King continued, "we will unite our bloodline with that of the Vassari Empire." The pride in my chest curdled instantly into something cold and horrifying. "Marriage?" The word escaped my lips before I could stop it, though thankfully too quietly for anyone but the nearby servants to hear. From the grand entrance, trumpets sounded. The massive doors swung open to reveal a woman I recognized from diplomatic portraits. Lady Isolde Vassari—tall, statuesque, with hair like spun gold and the confident bearing of someone who had never questioned her place in the world. "It is my great pleasure," Caden's voice rang out, steady and clear, "to announce my engagement to Lady Isolde Vassari, daughter of Emperor Konstantin. Our union will bring peace to our realms and prosperity to our people." The room erupted in applause. Glasses raised in celebration. Congratulations echoed from all sides. And I stood frozen, the world around me blurring as blood rushed in my ears. "But you're already married," a voice behind me murmured in confusion—some foreign dignitary unaccustomed to our kingdom's traditions. "The Verloren royal family practices diplomatic polygamy," came the explanation from someone else. "The first wife maintains her position, but second marriages are common for securing alliances." First wife. The words struck me like physical blows. I was being relegated to a footnote in my own marriage. Across the room, Caden's eyes finally found mine. For one breathless moment, I thought I saw regret there, perhaps even an apology. Then Lady Isolde touched his arm, and he turned to her with a smile I once believed belonged only to me. The realization crashed over me like ice water: I had never been a wife to him. I had been a treaty, a signature on parchment, a political arrangement disguised as a love match. And now that a better alliance had presented itself, I was simply being... supplemented.

body: “You ran from us once,” he murmured. “You won’t do it again.” Four princes want me. Two of them touched me in the dark and never forgot 🔥. One charms me like sunlight. One calms me like a lullaby. And the last two look at me like prey they already claimed 😰. Every dance, every hunt, every stolen moment tightens the noose around my neck. Because if they discover the truth growing inside me… the game won’t end in courtship. It will end in blood 🩸❤️‍🔥. Chapter 1 POV Eva The SUV cuts through the city at dusk, and I slump against the leather seats like something boneless, still flushed and sweaty from dance class.  My leotard clings uncomfortably beneath my jacket, a second skin I can't wait to shed. But that discomfort is nothing compared to the weight pressing down on my chest—the echo of my father's voice still ringing in my ears. Legacy. Bloodline.  Heirs responsibility of the werewolf dynasty, which they teach before you can walk. I've heard the sermon a thousand times. The same words, the same disappointment barely hidden beneath his measured tone. Twelve generations of Aldridge power, and it all rests on my shoulders. Wolves with barely glowing eyes grind against humans who don't know the danger pressed against them. The air is thick with sweat and alcohol and something wild.  Something primal that calls to the hollow space inside me. No one bows. No one stares. No one whispers behind their hands about the wolfless princess who should have been more. A stranger beside me signals for another round, and when the bartender delivers, the man pushes a second glass toward me with a lazy smile. I drink both glasses because I've never built a tolerance. Because my father would be horrified. Because tonight I don't care about the consequences, and maybe I never will again. I lean toward the bartender, emboldened by the warmth spreading through my chest. He's older, world-weary, the kind of man who's heard every confession the darkness has to offer. But I feel their presence like a physical force. The power radiating off them is unmistakable, pressing against my skin even from a distance. Alphas. His voice is silk and sin, some Mediterranean accent curling around the words. "You'll fall." "Maybe I want to fall," I hear myself say, bold in ways I've never been. His laugh is low and dark. "I've never been drunk before." I'm babbling now, the alcohol loosening my tongue. "Never been to a club. Never danced with strangers. I'm sandwiched between them before I register the movement, their bodies pressed against mine from front and back as we move to the bass.  The broader one's chest is a solid heat against my spine. The leaner one's fingers grip my hip bones like he's already claiming them.  I'm drowning in sensation, in the cedar and smoke of one scent mingling with the frost and pine of another, in the knowledge that two of the most powerful creatures in this room want me. The moment shatters when I spot Dominic. He's pushing through the crowd maybe twenty feet away, his face tight with panic. Dominic stands in the alley, chest heaving, eyes blazing amber—but he's too late. I'm already gone. A taxi idles at the curb like fate placed it there. The three of us pile inside, and I'm sandwiched between two wolves whose names I never asked. Whose faces I still can't quite focus on. Whose scents are wrapping around me like promises of everything I've been denied. The taxi pulls away from the curb just as the back door bursts open behind us. Dominic stands in the alley, chest heaving, eyes blazing amber—but he's too late. I'm already gone.  One moment of freedom starts a chain reaction that can’t be stopped ⛓️Read Princes’ Lover now and discover how one night of freedom changes everything 🔥

body:
“She’s just the first wife,” his sister said. “The new one will outshine her.”
 They thought I’d crumble when he announced his second marriage. Instead, I rose 🌹. For years, I swallowed their insults, their cruelty, the loneliness of a palace that never felt like home 💔. But I’m not the powerless girl they married off. I’m the daughter of King Dorian. The true heir to Verdana 🔥. And now that my crown is no longer hidden, I’m not here to beg for love—I’m here to take back everything they tried to steal 🔥👑.
 
 CHAPTER 1: 
 ELARA’S POV 
 The crystal champagne flute trembled in my hand as I watched my husband of three years move through the crowd like a predator among prey. Polished and pristine in his midnight blue formal attire, Prince Caden Verloren was the picture of royal perfection—a masterpiece I once believed I had some part in creating. "You've barely touched your drink, Your Highness," Lady Mirabelle whispered, her voice dripping with false concern. "Is something troubling you this evening?" I forced my lips into the practiced smile that had become as much a part of my royal wardrobe as the heavy crown that often adorned my head. "Not at all," I lied. "Just savoring the moment." What a bitter thing to savor. The Grand Hall of Verloren Palace glittered with more jewels and secrets than stars in the night sky. I'd grown accustomed to both during my years as Caden's wife, though neither brought me comfort anymore. "He looks especially handsome tonight," another courtier remarked, her eyes following my husband's movements with poorly disguised hunger. "The future king in his element." "Indeed," I replied, the single word nearly choking me. I remembered a time when Caden's smile had been reserved for me alone. When his hand would find mine beneath the table during tedious state dinners. When his eyes would seek mine across crowded rooms just like this one, a silent promise that we were in this together. Those days had vanished like morning mist before the summer sun. "Elara." My mother-in-law's voice cut through my thoughts. Queen Arielle approached with the silent grace of a viper. "Do fix your posture, dear. A queen never slumps." "I'm not a queen, Your Majesty," I reminded her, though I straightened my spine nonetheless. "Merely a princess." Her lips thinned to a bloodless line. "For now. But appearances matter at all times. Especially tonight." The cryptic addition sent a chill down my perfectly straight spine. "What's special about tonight?" I asked, trying to keep my voice light. The Queen's smile didn't reach her eyes. "You'll see soon enough." Before I could press further, she glided away, leaving me with a growing knot of dread in my stomach. I set down my untouched champagne and made my way toward Caden, determined to extract some explanation. I nearly reached him when the King's steward struck his staff against the marble floor three times. The resounding crack silenced the room immediately. "His Royal Majesty, King Darius Verloren the Third!" The crowd parted as my father-in-law entered, Caden immediately moving to stand beside him. My husband's eyes swept the room but passed over me as though I were merely another courtier. I felt my cheeks burn. "Valued guests and noble friends," the King began, his booming voice filling every corner of the vast hall. "We gather tonight not only to celebrate the prosperity of our kingdom but to share joyous news." My breath caught in my throat. Could it be the northern treaty? Caden had been working for months to secure peace with our traditional enemies. If successful, it would mean an end to centuries of bloodshed. The King's hand came to rest on Caden's shoulder with unusual affection. "Prince Caden has secured an alliance that will strengthen our realm for generations to come." Pride swelled in my chest momentarily. So he had done it. Despite the personal distance between us, I couldn't help but admire his diplomatic skill. "Through the ancient and honorable practice of royal marriage," the King continued, "we will unite our bloodline with that of the Vassari Empire." The pride in my chest curdled instantly into something cold and horrifying. "Marriage?" The word escaped my lips before I could stop it, though thankfully too quietly for anyone but the nearby servants to hear. From the grand entrance, trumpets sounded. The massive doors swung open to reveal a woman I recognized from diplomatic portraits. Lady Isolde Vassari—tall, statuesque, with hair like spun gold and the confident bearing of someone who had never questioned her place in the world. "It is my great pleasure," Caden's voice rang out, steady and clear, "to announce my engagement to Lady Isolde Vassari, daughter of Emperor Konstantin. Our union will bring peace to our realms and prosperity to our people." The room erupted in applause. Glasses raised in celebration. Congratulations echoed from all sides. And I stood frozen, the world around me blurring as blood rushed in my ears. "But you're already married," a voice behind me murmured in confusion—some foreign dignitary unaccustomed to our kingdom's traditions. "The Verloren royal family practices diplomatic polygamy," came the explanation from someone else. "The first wife maintains her position, but second marriages are common for securing alliances." First wife. The words struck me like physical blows. I was being relegated to a footnote in my own marriage. Across the room, Caden's eyes finally found mine. For one breathless moment, I thought I saw regret there, perhaps even an apology. Then Lady Isolde touched his arm, and he turned to her with a smile I once believed belonged only to me. The realization crashed over me like ice water: I had never been a wife to him. I had been a treaty, a signature on parchment, a political arrangement disguised as a love match. And now that a better alliance had presented itself, I was simply being... supplemented.
 
 Read My Husband Chose Her Over Me now to find out what happens next! 😱❤️‍🔥
